coredata.dc:title: Preparation of green luminescent silicon quantum dots by synergistic method for VB12 detection and antimicrobial property research application
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Liu, Huan
Zhang, Zhixia
Xiao, Xincai
Li, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The studies on the preparation of high-quality and long-wavelength-emitting silicon quantum dots and their application in biochemical detections have attracted increasing interests from researchers. In particular, the exploration of the effect of SiQDs and their complex in antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm areas, is of great significance to human health. This paper reports the fast synthesis of green-fluorescent SiQDs (TC-SiQDs, QYs=25%, Em=520 nm) with the synergistic effect of thiourea and catechol through microwave-assisted one-step hydrothermal route. Based on internal filtering effect, TC-SiQDs were then successfully employed as selective probes to detect vitamin B12, with detection range at 0.05–30 μM and limit of detection at 50 nM. Thanks to the excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence property of TC-SiQDs, they have been applied in bacterial imaging. The prepared TC-SiQDs also exhibit certain antibacterial ability with their minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) at 250 μg/mL, but their inhibitory ability is effective to the growth of S. aureus biofilm only when the concentration reaches 1.3 mg/mL. TC-SiQDs-lysozyme complex were prepared, based on the effects of singlet oxygen, charge effect and lysozyme hydrolysis of peptidoglycan in the cell wall, the complex exhibits high inhibiting activity with MIC against S. aureus at 10 μg/mL and exhibits superior inhibition activity against S. aureus biofilm at a concentration of only 62.5 μg/mL. This paper provides good theoretical and practical basis for the synthesis of long-wavelength emission and ultra-bright SiQDs and their applications in detection, antibacterial and biofilm growth inhibition, and other biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green light-emitting silicon quantum dots
Synergistic effect
Detection of VB12
Internal filtration effect
Antibacterial inhibition biofilm

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of green SiQDs and complexes of SiQDs and LZM
The impacts of synthesis environment upon the optical properties of prepared TC-SiQDs
Specific detection of VB12 by TC-SiQDs
TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM antibacterial inhibition biofilm and bacterial imaging applications
TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM antibacterial inhibition biofilm

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University ( CZY22003 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 2197832 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of fluorescence spectra of TC-SiQDs and SiQDs synthesized by different silicon sources.

(a) TEM images of TC-SiQDs, the left inset shows the corresponding HRTEM images (scale bar: 10 nm); the right inset shows the particle size distribution of TC-SiQDs, (b) XRD image of TC-SiQDs, (c) FTIR spectra of TC-SiQDs, High-resolution XPS spectra: (d) XPS survey scan, (e) N 1s XPS, (f) Si 2p XPS of TC-SiQDs.

(a) The emission spectra of TC-SiQDs after the addition of VB12 (from top to bottom: [VB12] = 0, 0.05, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 30 × 10−6 M), Inset: the standard curve of fluorescence intensity ratio before and after the presentation of VB12, (b) The overlap between absorption spectrum of VB12 and the emission spectra of TC-SiQDs.
Inhibition curves of S. aureus biofilm at 48 h (a) TC-SiQDs, (b) TC-SiQDs-LZM, (c) LZM.
Confocal laser microscopy images of S. aureus incubated with TC-SiQDs for 3 h at 37 °C (scale bar is 10 µm) (a) 557 nm, (b) 493 nm, (c) 353 nm, (d) bright field.
Schematic illustration of TC-SiQDs synthesized with reductant pair of catechol and thiourea.
Description of the mechanism of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM against various bacteria and inhibition of S. aureus biofilm.
MIC of TC-SiQDs, LZM and TC-SiQDs-LZM on various bacteria and MBIC of S. aureus biofilm.

head.ce:title: Preparation of green luminescent silicon quantum dots by synergistic method for VB12 detection and antimicrobial property research application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan 430065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central Minzu University), Wuhan 430065, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The studies on the preparation of high-quality and long-wavelength-emitting silicon quantum dots and their application in biochemical detections have attracted increasing interests from researchers. In particular, the exploration of the effect of SiQDs and their complex in antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm areas, is of great significance to human health. This paper reports the fast synthesis of green-fluorescent SiQDs (TC-SiQDs, QYs=25%, Em=520 nm) with the synergistic effect of thiourea and catechol through microwave-assisted one-step hydrothermal route. Based on internal filtering effect, TC-SiQDs were then successfully employed as selective probes to detect vitamin B12, with detection range at 0.05–30 μM and limit of detection at 50 nM. Thanks to the excellent biocompatibility and fluorescence property of TC-SiQDs, they have been applied in bacterial imaging. The prepared TC-SiQDs also exhibit certain antibacterial ability with their minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) at 250 μg/mL, but their inhibitory ability is effective to the growth of S. aureus biofilm only when the concentration reaches 1.3 mg/mL. TC-SiQDs-lysozyme complex were prepared, based on the effects of singlet oxygen, charge effect and lysozyme hydrolysis of peptidoglycan in the cell wall, the complex exhibits high inhibiting activity with MIC against S. aureus at 10 μg/mL and exhibits superior inhibition activity against S. aureus biofilm at a concentration of only 62.5 μg/mL. This paper provides good theoretical and practical basis for the synthesis of long-wavelength emission and ultra-bright SiQDs and their applications in detection, antibacterial and biofilm growth inhibition, and other biomedical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), as a fluorescence nanomaterial with easy availability, multiple synthesis routes, excellent optical properties, and low toxicity [1–3]. The development of a fast and simple one-step synthesis route for long-wavelength-emitting water-soluble SiQDs, and their further applications in biochemical detections and clinical fields, like imaging, drug delivery and treatment, are of great significance. The infection caused by pathogenic bacteria has seriously threatened food safety and human health, and thanks to their specific features in bacterial imaging and antibacterial abilities, SiQDs is an ideal candidate to infection treatment. For the present, the major problem in treating bacterial infections for antibiotics and nano antibacterial materials is the treatment of infection caused by bacterial biofilm, because it is complex microbial community attaching to the surface and encapsulated in extracellular matrix of microbial origin [4]. Biofilm matrix enables bacteria to tolerate harsh conditions and resist antimicrobial treatment [5]. To enhance the antibacterial ability of TC-SiQDs, we prepared TC-SiQDs-enzyme complex to study its ability in inhibiting biofilm. Recently, one-step hydrothermal route to prepare water-soluble SiQDs, under effect of reducing agents, has been widely accepted because of its simplicity and fastness without the requirement for complicated equipment [6]. However, most SiQDs prepared by one-step hydrothermal route are mostly blue-emitting [7]. Some research groups prepared green-emitting SiQDs through one-step route with proper selection of reducing agents, including catechol or glucose [8,9]. Though these methods can prepare green-emitting SiQDs, most products exhibit obvious drawbacks like low quantum yields (QYs) and weak optical stability, unbeneficial to the practical application in biomedical fields. And our team has developed a synthesis method for super-bright SiQDs based on the synergic effect [10,11]. The blue-emitting SiQDs were prepared with N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) as silicon source based on the synergic effect between double reducing agents citric and sodium oxalate, and the QYs of prepared SiQDs reached 80.10%. Considering the broader and more promising outlook of green-emitting SiQDs, by adjusting synthesis parameters, the fast and high-efficient preparation of green and yellow-emitting SiQDs were further developed with DAMO as the silicon source, and catechol and sodium citrate as double reducing agents. We will further investigate the synergistic effect of dual reducing agents in the preparation of high-performance green-emitting N and S-doped SiQDs.

As a novel fluorescence probe, SiQDs possess merits like fastness of detection and directness in detection results. It has been employed in the detection of various metal ions [12,13], H2O2 [14], proteins [15], anions [16], etc., playing important role in detecting biomedical materials. VB12, also known as cobalamin or cyanocobalamin, is one of the most important nutrients to maintain myelin sheath of nerve cells and many biochemical activities in human bodies [17]. VB12 is an essential organic compound for the body because of its role in forming red blood cells, and the production of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) in the process of cell division and the production of enzymes. Therefore, a sensitive and selective sensing platform of VB12 in drugs and biological samples, based on prepared novel long-wavelength emitting fluorescent silicon nanoparticles, has been successfully established.

Researchers are committed to the preparation and application of new antibacterial inorganic nanomaterials, such as nanogold, nanosilver, nanosilicon, quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), Silicon Nanomaterials, etc. [18]. For instance, Smirnov et al. proved that non-fluorescent Si nanoparticles’ ability in inhibiting Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [19]. Kafshgari and his team also proved that Si nanoparticles could effectively inhibit bacteria and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) biofilm [20]. Due to the low toxicity of Si, silicon-based nanomaterials show great application potential in biomedical fields. SiQDs exhibit excellent properties like good biocompatibility and excellent optical properties, but compared with other fluorescent nanomaterials, their applications in inhibiting bacteria and biofilm are still at the early stage, and no research on the direct use of SiQDs for biofilm inhibition has been found. Furthermore, to strengthen the antibacterial property of fluorescent nanomaterials, researchers have prepared a variety of high-efficient antibacterial materials through metal doping, coupling nanogold and nanosilver [21]. But the introduction of heavy metals during the preparation would lead to potential harm to their later clinical applications, and great impact on the fluorescence properties of the composite. Lysozyme (LZM) is a naturally occurring protein, alkaline enzyme that can hydrolyze mucopolysaccharides in pathogenic bacteria, and is non-toxic and non-harmful. Therefore, the combination of LZM and TC-SiQDs is selected in this paper, which can improve the antibacterial effect of TC-SiQDs film inhibition. The development of this new strong antibacterial film-inhibiting fluorescent composite is of great significance.

This research worked on the synthesis of green luminescent N,S-SiQDs with 25.0% QYs by using DAMO as silicon source and through synergic effect between the dual reducing agents thiourea and catechol. The impacts of various synthesis parameters, such as types of silicon sources, synthesis temperature, time and raw material ratio, on the fluorescent properties of prepared SiQDs are also investigated, and the mechanism for the synergic effect of double reducing agents during preparation was proposed. A series of characterization means have been employed to study the optical properties, surface groups and element composition of prepared SiQDs. Meanwhile, we found that SiQDs have high sensitivity and specificity for VB12 and performed linear detection of VB12. The detection mechanism was also explored as an internal filtration effect (IFE) based on the characterization of their optical properties and surface groups. Besides, the antibacterial ability tests showed that TC-SiQDs exhibit antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm abilities, and they have been successfully applied in bacterial imaging. This research provides a new perspective for the preparation of SiQDs, and their applications in sensing, antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm fields.

Thiourea (≥99.0%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Co., Ltd. N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]-ethylenediamine (DAMO) (95.0%), 3-ureidopropyltriethoxysilane (UPS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APS) (97.0%), catechol (≥ 99.0%) from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Amino acids was purchased from Biosharp Ltd. Sodium heparin was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Vitamin B12 (VB12) was purchased from Yunpeng Pharmaceutical Group Co., Ltd. Peptone (Oxoid Ltd) and Yeast (Oxoid Ltd) extract were purchased from Basingstoke, Hampshire, England, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), Lysozyme (LZM) and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide salt (MTT) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich., Rhodamine 6 G, ethanol, PBS buffer (20 mM, pH = 7), metal salts and other reagents were all analytically pure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of green SiQDs and complexes of SiQDs and LZM
ce:section.ce:para: Thiourea and DAMO were dissolved in 20 mL of water and stirred for 10 min, 2.2 mg catechol was added and stirred for 3 min. The product was then transferred to a microwave reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 30 min to obtain green-emitting TC-SiQDs. A solid sample of purified TC-SiQDs was obtained.

The solid sample of 100 mg TC-SiQDs was dissolved in 10 mL of water and stirred under light avoidance, 25 mg EDC was added and stirred for 10 min, 20 mg NHS was added and stirred for 30 min, 50 mg LZM was added and stirred for 3 h. The reaction was carried out in an oil bath at 37 °C for 24 h. The resulting complex was ultrafiltered in a 3000-ultrafiltration tube for 10 min. The solid sample of purified complex was obtained by freeze-drying.

835 μL of PBS buffer (20 mM, pH = 7) and 65 μL of TC-SiQDs (0.137 mg/mL) were mixed evenly. Then, 100 μL of different concentrations of VB12 was added to the above mixture to bring the total volume of the solution to 1 mL. Record their fluorescence spectrum changes at room temperature.

S. aureus, E. coli and P. aeruginosa were cultured in MH broth medium, B. subtilis was cultured in LB medium, and cultured overnight in a constant temperature shaking bed at the speed of 37 °C and 180 rpm. Biofilms were cultured in 96 well plates with TSB medium, and the bacterial concentration was determined by measuring the OD at 600 nm by UV–vis spectroscopy.

The MIC of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM was determined by the microdilution method in 96-well plates. 50 μL of bacterial suspension was adjusted to 2 × 107 CFU/mL, and 50 μL of bacterial suspension was added to 96-well plates. TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM were diluted in 96-well plates at a series of concentrations, with 50 μL of bacterial culture solution and 50 μL of each concentration of TC-SiQDs or TC-SiQDs-LZM in each well, totaling 100 μL. Subsequently, they were placed in a water-tight incubator and incubated for 12 h. Then, 5 μL of MTT was added and incubated again in a water-tight incubator for 30 min. After the incubation was completed, 100 μL of DMSO was added to develop the color and the experiment was repeated three times.

The biofilm of S. aureus was used as the target of inhibition, and the bacterial suspension was cultured. 50 μL of bacterial OD was adjusted to 5 × 107 CFU/mL with TSB medium and added to a 96-well plate. TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM were diluted at the desired concentrations, and the diluted drugs were added to a 96-well plate with 50 μL of bacterial suspension solution and 50 μL of each concentration of TC-SiQDs or TC-SiQDs-LZM solution in each well. The TSB medium plus bacterial suspension was used as the control, and no TC-SiQDs were added as the control group. The incubation was carried out in a water-tight incubator for 48 h. The absorbance of each well in the 96-well plate was measured every 2 h using an enzyme marker.

In the experiment, S. aureus was designated as the target bacteria, and the bacteria were taken in the medium and incubated in a constant temperature shaker at 37 °C for 12 h. 2 mL of bacterial suspension was removed and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the suspension was washed 2 times with PBS to remove the suspension. Afterward, 2 mL of TC-SiQDs solution was added to the PBS solution containing S. aureus and incubated for 3 h. After the incubation, the suspension was washed 2 times with PBS to remove the suspension and a small amount was placed on a slide. Finally, the bacteria were observed by confocal fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impacts of synthesis environment upon the optical properties of prepared TC-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of our previous research [9,22], to acquire long-emission wavelength SiQDs with high QYs, the impacts of silicon sources, raw material dosage, synthesis temperature and reaction time upon the optical properties of the prepared products have been systematically studied.

With other synthesis condition parameters fixed (reaction temperature 200 °C, reaction time 30 min, m(thiourea) = 0.6 mg, and m(catechol) = 2.2 mg), the impact of silicon source upon the optical properties of the products has been investigated by change of the type of silicon source. The three silicon sources, UPS [23], APS [24,25] and DAMO [26,27] are commonly used in SiQDs synthesis. As shown in Fig. 1, the fluorescence intensity of TC-SiQDs obtained with DAMO as the silicon source was much stronger than that of SiQDs obtained with APS and UPS as the silicon source, and the emission wavelength of SiQDs (DAMO) was also significantly longer. Comparatively, longer main chain structure of DAMO is easier in forming fluorescent structures, proving the critical role of silicon source in SiQDs synthesis.

Similarly, with other synthesis environment parameters fixed, the impacts of two reducing agents’ dosages were also studied. As shown in Fig. S1(a), the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs reaches strongest when the amount of thiourea is 1.8 mg, but the slightly increase or decreased amount of thiourea does not set great impact upon the fluorescence intensity of the product. The amount of catechol, however, greatly influenced the optical properties of prepared TC-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S1(b), when the addition of catechol increased from 0.6 mg to 4.4 mg, the emission wavelength of prepared TC-SiQDs gradually red-shifts from 511 nm to 533 nm, showing the effective doping of the large conjugate group of catechol into the structure of TC-SiQDs. The fluorescence intensity of the product reaches maximum when the addition amount of catechol is 2.2 mg.

The reaction time and temperature in hydrothermal route also set great impact upon the optical properties of the product. As shown in Fig. S1(c) and Fig. S1(d), the optimal time at 30 min and temperature is 200 °C. The low reaction temperature leads to slow dehydration of silane, unbeneficial to crystal core formation and following dehydration and carbonization process, producing defects on the surface of prepared product. The short reaction time would make the incomplete synthesis process of SiQDs. On the other hand, overheating or excessive reaction time would lead to over-carbonization of SiQDs and intensify the side-effect of the self-conjugation of silane, greatly reducing the fluorescence intensity of the product. Meanwhile, the low reaction temperature or reaction time makes insufficient interaction between two reducing agents, unbeneficial to the formation of crystal core and perfect crystal shape, resulting in low fluorescence intensity of the products.

The experiment results showed that with the reaction time increased from 5 min to 40 min, the emission wavelength of TC-SiQDs blue-shifted from 541 nm to 519 nm (Fig. S1(c)), while the reaction temperature increasing from 110 °C to 200 °C, the emission peak blue-shifted from 545 nm to 519 nm (Fig. S1(d)). The possible reason might be that the poor thermal stability of catechol under high reaction temperature and long reaction time would destroy catechol structure, interfere its function in forming luminescent surface groups of TC-SiQDs, and thus lead to shorter emission wavelength of prepared SiQDs.

Through a series of experiments, the optical synthesis environment are found to be 1.8 mg thiourea, 2.2 mg catechol and 0.4 mL DAMO at 200 °C for 30 min, with QYs of prepared at 25.0%. The product exhibits green fluorescence under UV light. Fluorescence spectrology and UV–vis spectrology were used to assess the optical properties of TC-SiQDS. As shown in Fig. S2, under optimal excitation wavelength 420 nm, the emission peak of TC-SiQDs is at 520 nm. Besides absorption peak at 428 nm, SiQDs also have two shoulder peak at 294 nm and 242 nm, attributing to the π→π * transition of CN or CC bond [28] and to the π→π * electron transfer of N containing carbon ring respectively.

As shown in the TEM image of TC-SiQDs ( Fig. 2(a)), the average particle size on the surface is at 6–8 nm, and HRTEM image (left insert of Fig. 2(a)) shows that SiQDs possess good crystallinity with lattice spacing at 0.30 nm. XRD result showed that the X-ray diffraction spectrum was scanning between 2θ 15°− 75° (Fig. 2(b)), and there appeared a single diffraction peak at 2θ= 21.9°, corresponding to the 101 plane of the silicon oxide crystal structure (PDF#82–1403 SiO2), which is in accordance to the results reported in references [29]. FTIR was used to explore the surface groups of synthesized TC-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the shoulder peak at 3366 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H bond. The absorption peaks at 3275 and 1578 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H bond and the bending vibration of N-H, and the peak at 775 cm−1 to the waving vibration of N-H. The peaks at 2940 and 1474 cm−1 are attributed to the saturated tensile vibration and bending vibration of C-H bond. The peak at 1310 cm−1 attributing to asymmetric deformation of Si-C can also be observed. The absorption at 1124–1027 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of TC-SiQDs. At 691 cm−1, the peak belong to secondary amine was also observed. The existences of all these surface functional groups are beneficial to the excellent water-solubility and stability of prepared TC-SiQDs.

The surface components and elements of prepared TC-SiQDs were analyzed with help of XPS. There are five peaks at 102.4, 152.7, 284.8, 399.2 and 531.0 eV in the XPS spectra of TC-SiQDs, corresponding to Si, S, C, N and O, respectively (Fig. 2(d)). The high resolution spectrum of C 1s reveals the existence of four types of carbon atoms: C-Si, C-C, C-N and C-O/C-S, corresponding to the peaks at 284.1, 284.6, 285.2 and 286.1 eV respectively (Fig. S3(a)) [30]. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2(e)) shows three peaks at 399.6, 398.3 and 398.8 eV, corresponding to N-C, N-Si and N-H, respectively [31]. The peak of O 1s in XPS has three subpeaks at 531.4, 531.9, 532.4 eV, corresponding to O-S, O-Si, and C-OH respectively (Fig. S3(b)) [32,33]. There are three peaks at 101.0, 101.8, 102.5 eV in Si 2p spectrum (Fig. 2(f)), attributing to the existence of Si-C, Si-N and Si-O groups [31,34]. The peak at 167.3 eV in S 2p spectrum corresponds to SO group (Fig. S3(c)) [35]. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) pattern of TC-SiQDs indicated that SiQDs contain C, O, N, Si and S elements (Fig. S4(a)), with each content at 62.94%, 17.82%, 10.84%, 8.17% and 0.22% respectively. Since the carrier selected during the tests was copper mesh carbon film, the unidentified peak on the right is the energy spectrum peak of Cu. As shown in Fig. S4(b), the nitrogen absorption/desorption curve of TC-SiQDs belongs to IV isotherm, and its hysteresis loop is the H3 type, which is the feature of mesoporous materials [36]. The specific surface area of BET is 2.8985 m2/g.

The dual reducing agents sodium oxalate and citric acid have been applied in the preparation of SiQDs based on their synergic effect when using DAMO as silicon source. The QYs of the product have been greatly enhanced without emission wavelength shifting. Comparatively, the presence of single reducing agent catechol would produce green-emitting products, and the QYs was extremely low, less stable and fluorescence decreased rapidly with storage time. On the other hand, when using thiourea as the single reducing agent, the prepared product was blue-emitting. The presence of proper double reducing agents catechol and thiourea during the synthesis process could greatly enhanced the QYs of prepared product with redshifts of emission wavelength, proving the beneficial effect of synergic effect between the two reducing agents. Scheme 1.

With the presence of reducing agent, the silicon source is reduced in high-temperature aqueous solution, and forms large Si-O-Si aggregates. The existence of high-efficient reducing agent can further accelerate the formation of crystal core and reduce the surface defects of the product. CA exhibits strong reducing ability, and is the most commonly used reducing agent during the preparation of super-bright blue-emitting SiQDs [37]. But the reducing agents used in this paper (catechol and thiourea) both exhibited weaker reducing ability than that of CA, and the presence of any one of them would produce quite limited reducing behavior to the silicon source, and their products both have pretty low QYs. However, the presence of both reducing agents in the preparation proved the existence of synergic effect, when their reducing abilities are complemented and promoted to produce SiQDs with much higher QYs.

Besides their reducing effect to silicon source during the synthesis process, the active groups in catechol and thiourea can further form large copolymers with Si-O-Si aggregates, and the phenolic hydroxyl groups in catechol, which is at the ortho position of the benzene ring, can easily combine with the residues on the surface of DAMO hydrolysate, forming fluorophores with large conjugated system and carbon-oxygen structure and then connecting onto Si-O-Si aggregate. The high O/C ratio in IR spectra also confirmed carbonyl groups on the surface of prepared SiQDs. The existence of thiourea realizes the N and S elements doping into SiQDs, promoting formation of new surface state, changing the electronic band gap on the surface, and reducing surface defects of prepared SiQDs as well as the possibility of non-radiation, beneficial to the enhancement of QYs. Furthermore, the great content of carbon element in silicon dot and the doped S element would promise the forming of =CS, enhancing the sp2/sp3 ratio of C atoms, and thus improving the fluorescence properties of the product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific detection of VB12 by TC-SiQDs

ce:section.ce:para: As a water-soluble vitamin, VB12 plays crucial role in many biochemical reactions and is an essential substance for human body. As shown in Fig. S5, it was discovered that VB12 exhibited excellent quenching ability to the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs at the concentration as low as 1.00 × 10−4 M with the fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F) at 4.13. The impacts of cations, anions, essential amino acids of human body, and other common amino acids and drugs upon the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs have been tested. The quenching rates of these substances are all at about 1, showing their inability in impacting QDs fluorescence and consequently the high selectivity of TC-SiQDs to VB12.

To realize the specific and sensitive detection of VB12, the optimization of detection environment is needed. The photostability of TC-SiQDs was firstly investigated. Under the irradiation of UV light (365 nm) for 30 min, the fluorescence intensity of TC-SiQDs remained stable. As shown in Fig. S6, after the addition of VB12, the fluorescence reached stable in 5 min. So, all the detections are carried out 5 min after the interaction between VB12 and TC-SiQDs.

The pH value of the solution is also an important factor during the detection, which would influence the fluorescence intensity of TC-SiQDs. As shown in Fig. S7(a), the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs stays stable with pH value ranging in 4–10, and after the addition of 5.00 × 10−5 M VB12, the quenching ratios of TC-SiQDs’s fluorescence in different buffer systems (pH 3–14) are quite similar (Fig. S7(b)). Considering the further application in biological environment, the phosphate with pH at 7.4 was chosen as the buffer for following detections.

As shown in Fig. 3(a), when fixing TC-SiQDs concentration at 0.14 mg/mL, with VB12 concentration increasing from 50 nM to 30 μM, the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs keeps decreasing, which fits the Eq. (1) Stern-Volmer equation:
(1) F0/F = 1 + Ksv [c]

where F0 and F are the intensities of TC-SiQDs fluorescence before and after the addition of VB12, Ksv is the quenching constant (7.42 × 105 M), which is calculated via fitting curve equation, [c] is VB12 concentration. The results showed that VB12 concentration and the fluorescence intensity ratio of TC-SiQDs keep good linear relationship with detection range in 50 nM to 30 μM. The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9969, and limit of detection is 50 nM.

As VB12 sensor, TC-SiQDs exhibit obvious advantages than other nanomaterials (N,S-CQDs, CDs-Ws, SiQDs, CQDs, Y-CDs, TSA/BSA-Au/AgNCs), and show better optical properties (Table. S1). In addition, in detecting VB12, TC-SiQDs show lower limit of detection and wider detection range than most nanomaterials.

In recent years, researchers have designed and developed various fluorescence sensing methods based on fluorescence quenching process, utilizing quenching mechanisms like static quenching effect, dynamic quenching effect, photoinduced electron transfer, foster resonance energy transfer, electron energy transfer, IFE, surface energy transfer and intramolecular charge transfer, etc. [38]. Based on the studies on the structures and optical properties of TC-SiQDs and VB12, the mechanism based on IFE in the sensitive and specific detection of VB12 is proposed. As shown in Fig. 3(b), VB12 has a wide absorption peak at 450–600 nm area in its UV spectra, greatly overlapping the emission spectrum of TC-SiQDs (emission wavelength at 520 nm). The overlapping area takes about 89% of the emission spectra of TC-SiQDs, showing that the quenching behavior of VB12 to the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs is caused by IFE, in which VB12 as the absorber could effectively absorb the emission of TC-SiQDs, thus leading to the quenching of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM antibacterial inhibition biofilm and bacterial imaging applications
TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM antibacterial inhibition biofilm
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC of TC-SiQDs against various pathogens was evaluated through broth microdilution method. As shown in Table 1, the minimum concentrations of TC-SiQDs against the growth of S. aureus, B. subtilis, E. coli and P. aeruginosa are 250 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, 700 μg/mL and 450 μg/mL, respectively, but their ability against S. aureus biofilm is pretty weak. As shown in Fig. 4(a), only when the concentration of TC-SiQDs reaches 1250 μg/mL can the growth of S. aureus biofilm be effectively inhibited. When the concentration drops to 625 μg/mL, the growth and formation of S. aureus biofilm can be inhibited in the first 10 h, and after then, the biofilm grows rapidly.

To solve the problem of poor sensitivity in antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm activities of SiQDs, the TC-SiQDs-LZM with high antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm ability was prepared by activating -OH bound in TC-SiQDs through coupling LZM on the surface of SiQDs. The MIC results of TC-SiQDs-LZM complex in broth microdilution method are listed in Table 1. The MIC against S. aureus and B. subtilis are 10 μg/mL, but TC-SiQDs-LZM complex exhibit almost no inhibitory activity to Gram-negative bacteria. The prepared complex shows enhanced antibacterial ability and selectivity, and their antibacterial mechanism will be investigated in the following part.

It is worth noticing that TC-SiQDs-LZM have strong inhibitory ability against S. aureus biofilm (Fig. 4(b)). When the concentration of TC-SiQDs-LZM complex reaches 15.6 μg/mL, the complex could inhibit the growth of S. aureus biofilm for 35 h, and when the concentration increases to 62.5 μg/mL, they could inhibit the biofilm growth for 48 h. The cytotoxicity was determined by Cell viability tests, and BV2 cells were cultured with different concentrations of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM for 24 h, and then the cell survival rate was measured. The results are shown in Fig. S8. The cell survival rates of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM at 150 μg/mL were 90.06% and 91.50%, respectively, indicating that TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM are biocompatible. Meanwhile, TC-SiQDs-LZM showed lower cytotoxicity than TC-SiQDs, indicating that the addition of the enzyme resulted in improved biocompatibility of TC-SiQDs. The low cytotoxicity of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM ensures their potential application in biomarkers or bioimaging.

The excellent optical properties of TC-SiQDs also mean their application as probe in bacterial imaging. S. aureus was cultivated with the presence of TC-SiQDs, and then observed under confocal fluorescence microscope. Fig. 5 shows the images of S. aureus. Under the excitation of 557, 493 and 353 nm lasers, S. aureus exhibits strong orange, green and blue fluorescence (Fig. 5(a), (b), (c)), and the clear spherical structure of S. aureus can be observed, showing S. aureus could intake TC-SiQDs.

The antibacterial and biofilm inhibition mechanisms of TC-SiQDs and TC-SiQDs-LZM have been investigated. The oxidation experiment of SOSG under the presence of 1O2 shows that TC-SiQDs could produce singlet oxygen, as shown in Fig. S9. Based on the literature [39] and our previous work [40], it has been proven that singlet oxygen can cause serious oxidative damage to the bacterial cell membrane and finally bacterial death. The surface charge of nanoparticle also plays important role during their antibacterial process. As shown in Fig. S10(a), TC-SiQDs are negatively charged with zeta potential at about − 13.5 ± 3.5 eV, leading to weak binding with also negatively charged bacterial, and thus poor antibacterial sensitivity. Scheme 2.

The excellent antibacterial property, especially its biofilm inhibition ability, of prepared TC-SiQDs-LZM complex originates from SiQDs itself, but also from the antibacterial ability of LZM. Firstly, LZM is an alkaline enzyme that can hydrolyze mucopolysaccharide in pathogenic bacteria. It could destroy the β-1,4 glycosidic bonds between N-acetylmuramic acid and N-acetylglucosamine in the cell wall to turn the insoluble mucopolysaccharide of the cell wall into soluble glycopeptides [41], leading to the rupture of the cell wall, escape of the content and finally the dissolution of bacteria. TC-SiQDs-LZM complex maintain the glycosidase function of LZM, and shows strong antibacterial ability to Gram-positive bacteria, but no antibacterial activity against Gram-negative bacteria. That is because the cell of Gram-positive bacteria is almost composed of peptidoglycan, while for Gram-negative bacteria, only the inner layer of cell wall is made of peptidoglycan, and there is a lipopolysaccharide (LPS) layer on its cell wall. This LPS layer could protect bacteria from LZM [42]. Therefore, LZM shows higher sensitivity Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria [43].

Besides, the surface of LZM is positively charged with its zeta potential at + 19.7 ± 3.6 eV, as shown in Fig. S10(c). After its coupling with TC-SiQDs, the TC-SiQDs-LZM complex are still positively charged with zeta potential at + 15.9 ± 4.1 eV, as shown in Fig. S10(b). Because Gram-positive bacteria is negatively charged, the positively-charged TC-SiQDs-LZM complex could strongly bind onto the bacteria, beneficial to the membrane breaking damage to the bacteria.

As to the inhibition of S. aureus biofilm, the weak inhibitory ability of TC-SiQDs originates from the extracellular polymer (EPS) produced by S. aureus biofilm, which could protect the biofilm from the damage of TC-SiQDs and thus prevent it from effectively inhibit the growth of biofilm. Hukic M. at al. proved that LZM could inhibit the growth of bacterial biofilm [44] because LZM could attach and accumulate onto the surface of negatively charged biofilm, inducing more and larger holes on the cell wall, changing the permeability of the membrane, and finally the bacterial lysis and death. S. aureus biofilm is composed by extracellular polysaccharide matrix and biofilm related proteins. Since polysaccharide is the main component of microbial adhesive, glycosidase function of TC-SiQDs-LZM complex could specifically destroy the ester chain of polysaccharide, and inhibit the formation of biofilm by reducing the adhesion strength of spores, and thus show strong biofilm inhibition ability.

This paper reports the preparation of green-emitting TC-SiQDs (λem = 520 nm, QYs = 25.0%) with silane couple agent DAMO and under the synergistic effect of reducing agents thiourea and catechol. Several characterization means, including IR, XPS, and zeta potential tests have been used to characterize the element contents and surface properties of prepared TC-SiQDs. The preparation mechanism is also investigated. Based on the IFE, VB12 could effectively quench the fluorescence of TC-SiQDs, and the sensitive and specific detection method of VB12 has been realized with detection range at 50 nM-30 μM and detection limit at 50 nM. Due to the singlet oxygen damage effect, the prepared TC-SiQDs also exhibit certain antibacterial and inhibition of biofilm activity with MIC against S. aureus at 250 μg/mL, and the multiple-color imaging of S. aureus is also realized. TC-SiQDs-LZM complex was prepared to enhance the biofilm inhibition ability because the complex surface is positively charged and exhibits hydrolysis function of glycosidase. The MIC of the complex against S. aureus reaches 10 μg/mL and MBIC against S. aureus biofilm reaches 62.5 μg/mL, showing their great potential as antibacterial nano complex. This research provides a fast and simple means for preparation of long-wavelength-emitting SiQDs, but also expands their application in drug detection and biomedical fields.

Dan Zhao: Writing − original draft, Writing − review & editing. Huan Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Zhixia Zhang: Investigation. Xincai Xiao, Jun Li: Conceptualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University (CZY22003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (2197832).

ce:section.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112868.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of lysozyme on reducing biofilms by Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Gardnerella vaginalis: an in vitro examination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Mirsada, S. Dzenita, R. Amra, I.M. Avdić, D. Serkan, H. Jasna, B.E. Feric, The effect of lysozyme on reducing biofilms by Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Gardnerella vaginalis: an in vitro examination, Microb. Drug Resist., 24 (2018), pp. 353–358, https://doi.org/10.1089/mdr.2016.0303.
